FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly states use of first wave of ABCD study, 9-10 year olds, N=3847 after QC

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; machine learning regression (LASSO, elastic net, ridge) on 43 variables predicting BMI

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Psychological Medicine, 2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time defined as "average minutes per week a child typically spends on a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device" — this is multi-device aggregated time with no per-device breakdown isolating phone or SM, violating the composite-screen-time rule

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because screen time is measured as a multi-device composite (computer + cellphone + tablet + other) without breakdown isolating phone or SM. Although the authors report screen time as a significant correlate of BMI, it does not meet the conceptual definition of a qualifying SM/phone variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper states: "This study utilized the baseline cross-sectional survey administered within the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD; Release 2.0)." ABCD recruited over 11,000 U.S. children age 9-10 across 21 study sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis. Children reported screen media use; parents completed reports of sleep disturbance severity. Logistic regressions reported SM/phone associations with sleep disturbance outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Sleep Health, 2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Children separately reported use of TV, video, video game, social media, texting, and video chat. Social media, texting (on phone), and video chat are qualifying SM/phone variables. Paired with individual-trait-level DV: sleep disturbance severity (including insomnia, sleep-wake transition disturbances, excessive sleepiness, etc.)

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regressions reported odds ratios for SM/phone variables predicting sleep disturbances. E.g., "Greater screen media use will be associated with greater symptom severity of insomnia"; results show ORs such as 1.23 [1.05-1.47] for social media use with insomnia, 1.27 [1.08-1.50] for video game use with insomnia (Table 2)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results throughout: multiple odds ratios (OR 1.23-1.56 range) with 95% CIs reported for associations between social media, texting, video chat, and sleep disturbance symptom severity in Tables 2 and 3

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional, not longitudinal; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal SM-as-DV-only designs
notes: Paper clearly qualifies. Distinct SM/phone variables (social media, texting, video chat) are IV; sleep disturbance severity is DV (individual-trait-level outcome). Cross-sectional design. Multiple numeric results reported.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states: "Childhood suicidal ideation and behaviours are poorly understood. We examined correlates of suicidality in a US population-based sample of children participating in the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) study." Sample comprises 7994 children, mean age 9.9 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: bootstrapped logistic regression to quantify association between suicidal ideation/behaviors and multiple predictor domains (mental and physical wellbeing, behavior, cognition, family environment)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in The Lancet Psychiatry, 2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions: "The risk of child-reported suicidality increased with higher weekend screen use time (OR 1.3, 95% CI 1.2-1.7)." This is composite "screen use" without per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM. No evidence in abstract of separately measured SM or phone variables paired with suicidality.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2. No PDF available; abstract shows generic "screen use time" associated with suicidality but does not isolate phone or SM from composite screen-use measure. Does not meet the conceptual requirement for a qualifying SM/phone variable.
